title: The relationship between chronic fatigue and unprocessed trauma
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
You're not lazy. You're exhausted in ways that make sense.

I've been there. That bone-deep tiredness that sleep doesn't touch. The kind where waking up feels like you've already run a marathon. Where your limbs are heavy, your brain is foggy, and the thought of a shower feels like climbing Everest.

For years, I thought I was broken. I tried more sleep, better sleep, supplements, diets, exercise. Nothing worked. Or it worked for a few days, then the exhaustion came crashing back. Doctors ran tests. Thyroid, iron, vitamin D, all normal. "Maybe it's stress," they'd say, and send me home with a pamphlet about sleep hygiene.

Here's the thing. They weren't wrong about the stress. But they missed the real story. The stress wasn't just in my head. It was in my nervous system. It was stored in my body. And my chronic fatigue wasn't a deficiency of sleep or willpower. It was the cost of living in a body that never felt safe.

I want to be really clear about something. I'm not saying all chronic fatigue is caused by trauma. There are real medical conditions that need real medical attention. But if you've been through the tests, tried the protocols, and still feel like you're dragging your body through each day, it's worth looking at what your nervous system might be carrying.

Because your body isn't betraying you. It's trying to protect you. And that protection comes at a metabolic price.

What your nervous system is actually doing when you're exhausted

Let's get into the science for a minute. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. It's not a conscious choice. It's happening below the level of awareness, in the architecture of your body.

When your nervous system detects threat, it mobilizes you to fight or flee. That takes energy. A lot of energy. Adrenaline, cortisol, heart rate up, blood sugar mobilized. You're primed for action. But here's the part that matters for chronic fatigue. When fight or flight isn't possible, or when the threat never ends, your nervous system has another option. It collapses. It shuts down. It goes into what Porges calls dorsal vagal shutdown.

That's the freeze response. The one that makes you feel numb, dissociated, and profoundly tired. The one that makes your body feel heavy, your mind slow, your motivation nonexistent. Sound familiar?

Think about it. If your nervous system has been running on high alert for years, it's going to burn out. The tank runs dry. And the only way your body knows to conserve energy is to power down non-essential systems. Digestion slows. Reproduction slows. Healing slows. And your conscious experience of that shutdown is exhaustion.

I remember reading Bessel van der Kolk's work on how trauma reshapes the body and brain. He talks about how the body keeps the score, even when the mind has forgotten. A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). It's not an easy read, but it helped me understand that my fatigue wasn't a personal failing. It was a physiological response to an impossible situation.

Stay with me here. Because this is where it gets both harder and more hopeful. The exhaustion isn't just from the past. It's from the present moment. Your nervous system is still running the same survival programs it learned years ago. It's still bracing for impact. Still waiting for the other shoe to drop. And that constant vigilance, even if you're not consciously aware of it, is metabolically expensive.

You're not tired because you're broken. You're tired because you've been fighting a war that ended a long time ago, and your body doesn't know it's over yet.

The connection between chronic fatigue and the freeze response

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, describes the freeze response as the body's last resort. When fight and flight are impossible, when you're trapped or overwhelmed, your nervous system goes into a state of immobility. It's the same response you see in a deer that plays dead when a predator is near. The deer isn't resting. It's in a state of physiological shutdown. Heart rate drops, breathing slows, pain sensitivity decreases. The body is conserving every last drop of energy because it doesn't know if it will survive the next few minutes.

Here's the problem. For many of us with unprocessed trauma, we never fully come out of that freeze state. We're living in a partial shutdown. The predator is gone, but the nervous system doesn't know that. So it stays in conservation mode. And that conservation mode feels exactly like chronic fatigue.

I've had clients describe it as being underwater. Everything is slow, heavy, muffled. They can hear the world above, but they can't reach it. They're trapped in their own bodies. And the harder they try to push through, the more exhausted they become. Because pushing through is fight energy. And if the nervous system is already in freeze, fight just creates more internal conflict.

You can't will yourself out of a freeze state. You can't positive-affirmation your way out of chronic fatigue that's rooted in trauma. The body needs to feel safe enough to come back online. And that safety has to be felt in the body, not just understood in the mind.

I want to offer you something practical. When you notice that bone-deep exhaustion, try this. Don't fight it. Don't push through. Instead, ask your body one question. "What do you need right now?" And then wait. Don't force an answer. Just wait. Maybe it's a weighted blanket on your chest. A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Gabor Mate's work on this is essential reading. Maybe it's a slow exhale. A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). Something as simple as extending your exhale can signal safety to your vagus nerve.

Maybe it's just lying down and letting your body be heavy. Without guilt. Without shame. Without the inner critic telling you you're wasting time. Because rest that comes from a place of safety is different from collapse that comes from exhaustion. One heals. The other just keeps the cycle going.

Why the wellness industry gets this wrong

I need to be direct with you about something. The wellness industry has done a lot of damage to people with chronic fatigue. The message is always the same. Do more. Try harder. Detox, cleanse, , biohack. If you're still tired, you're not doing it right. You're not disciplined enough. You're not committed enough.

That's toxic positivity dressed up as self-care. And it's deeply harmful for people whose fatigue is rooted in trauma.

Look, I'm not against healthy habits. I eat well. I move my body. I prioritize sleep. But none of those things healed my chronic fatigue. What helped was understanding that my body had been in survival mode for decades. What helped was learning to listen to my nervous system instead of overriding it. What helped was stopping the war with my own body.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The gentle pressure can help your body feel contained and safe. It's not a cure. Nothing is. But it's a tool that can help your nervous system start to shift out of that chronic freeze state.

Here's what I want you to take away from this. Your chronic fatigue is not a character flaw. It's not a lack of willpower. It's not something you need to fix by trying harder. It's a message from your nervous system. A message that says, "I've been carrying too much for too long. I need to feel safe. I need to rest. I need to be held."

And that's not weakness. That's wisdom. Your body has been trying to protect you the only way it knows how. It's time to listen. Not to fix. Not to force. Just to listen.

Because the fatigue isn't the enemy. It's the signal. And when you start following that signal, you might just find the path home to your own body.

Sit with that.
